content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 7 - Chapter 10 – “P” for Place: Customer value fulfilmentPart 4 – Retailing and Wholesaling.
Chapter 10 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 4 – Retailing and Wholesaling.

Retailing (1 of 5)
Retailing refers to all activities involved in selling goods or services directly to final consumers for their personal, non-business use.
Retailing: Connecting brands with consumers
Shopper marketing refers to using the entire marketing process, from product to logistics and promotion, to extend brand equity to ‘the last mile’ and encourage favourable point-of-purchase decisions.
Omni-channel retailing involves creating a seamless cross-channel buying experience that integrates in-store, online and mobile shopping.
Q: Are you an omni-channel shopper? What are some benefits and disadvantages of omni-channel retailing for (a) the shopper and (b) the retailer?

Retailing (2 of 5)
Retailing: Connecting brands with consumers
Types of retailers
Retailers can be classified by:
amount of service offered (e.g. self-service, limited service and full service)
product line sold (e.g. length and breadth of their product assortments; ‘product line’ can be a service (e.g. hotel, bank)
relative prices charged (e.g. discount store, warehouse clubs)
how they are organised (e.g. corporate chains, voluntary chains, retailer cooperatives, franchise organisations).

Table 10.1
Major store retailer types (abridged)
Type
Description
Examples
Speciality stores
Carry a narrow product line with a deep assortment, e.g. apparel stores, sporting-goods stores, furniture stores, florists, bookstores.
Department stores
Carry several production lines (e.g. clothing, home furnishings, household goods) with each line operated as a separate department.
Supermarkets
Relatively large, low-cost, low-margin, high-volume, self-service operations carrying grocery and household products.
Convenience stores
Relatively small stores located near residential areas, open long hours 7 days a week and carrying a limited line of high-turnover products at slightly higher prices.
Discount stores
Carry standard merchandise sold at lower prices with lower margins and higher volumes.
Mass merchants
Carry a large assortment of home and home-improvement merchandise, including DIY hardware items.
Off-price retailers
Sell merchandise bought at less-than-regular wholesale prices and sold at less than retail prices (e.g. leftover goods, seconds, overruns).
Superstores
Very large stores traditionally aimed at meeting consumers’ total needs for routinely purchased food and non-food items
Q: Give examples of each of these retailer types.

Retailing (4 of 5)
Retailer marketing decisions
Retailers are always searching for new marketing strategies to attract and hold customers.
Retailers face major marketing decisions about their:
segmentation, targeting, differentiation and positioning
product and service assortment
price
promotion
placement
people, digital processes and physical evidence.
Q: What are some of the new challenges confronting today’s retailers?

Figure 10.8
Retailer marketing decisions

Wholesaling (1 of 8)
Wholesaling includes all the activities involved in selling goods and services to those buying for resale or business use.
A wholesaler is a firm engaged primarily in wholesaling activity.
Q: How do wholesalers differ from retailers?

Wholesaling (2 of 8)
Wholesalers add value by performing one or more channel functions:
selling and promoting
buying and assortment building
bulk breaking
warehousing
transportation
financing
risk bearing
management services and advice.
Q: Some wholesalers ‘retail’ direct to consumers. Can you think of some examples?

Wholesaling (3 of 8)
Types of wholesalers
Wholesalers fall into three main groups:
merchant wholesalers
agents and brokers
manufacturers’ sales branches and offices.

Wholesaling (4 of 8)
Types of wholesalers (cont.)
Merchant wholesalers are independently owned business that take title to the merchandise they handle.
Agents and brokers do not take title to goods.
Agents represent buyers (or sellers) on a more permanent basis.
Brokers bring buyers and sellers together to assist in negotiations.

Table 10.2
Main types of wholesalers (abridged)
Type
Examples
Merchant wholesalers
Independently owned businesses that take title to the merchandise they handle
Full-service wholesalers
Wholesale merchants; Industrial distributors
Limited-service wholesalers
Cash-and-carry wholesalers; Truck wholesalers; Rack jobbers; Producers’ cooperatives; Mail-order or web wholesalers
Brokers and agents
Do not take title to goods; perform only a few functions
Brokers
Food broker; Real estate broker; Insurance broker
Agents
Manufacturers’ agents; Selling agents; Purchasing agents; Commission merchants
Manufacturers’ sales branches and offices
Wholesaling operations conducted by sellers or buyers themselves
Sales branches and offices
In industries such as timber; car equipment and parts
Purchasing offices
Similar role to broker or agent but part of the buyer’s organisation

Wholesaling (6 of 8)
Wholesaler marketing decisions
As with retailers, wholesalers’ marketing decisions include:
segmentation, targeting, differentiation and positioning decisions
marketing mix decisions, i.e. product and service assortment; price; promotion; placement; and people, processes and physical evidence.

Figure 10.9
Wholesaler marketing decisions

Wholesaling (8 of 8)
Wholesaling trends and developments
Trends and developments include:
need for ever-greater efficiency
consolidation
geographical expansion
information technology and automated systems
international competition.
Q: What are some other wholesaling trends and developments? Give examples.

Retailing and Wholesaling
A:
A: Omni-channel shopping: benefits to the shopper: it is a quick way to gather information across various retailers and brands and compare prices and availability. The advantage to the retailer is that they are exposed to a large number of new customers. Disadvantages to shoppers: maybe they end up with too much choice and if they do not go into a bricks-and-mortar store they may not get consultative advice from informed salespeople. The disadvantages to retailers: comparison shopping leads to pressure to reduce prices to meet the competition. Online shopping means that retailers are exposed to more competition from competing retailers out-of-town and even out-of-country. This means that retailers need to price match and by provide “click & collect” services and other inducements.
Q: Are you an omni-channel shopper? What are some benefits and disadvantages of omni-channel retailing for (a) the shopper and (b) the retailer?

Retailing and Wholesaling
Q: What are some of the new challenges confronting today’s retailers?
A: need to invest in: information systems, warehousing automation, systems for direct-delivery from producer to store, packaging methods that mean that once the product is packed by the supplier that package simply needs to be opened on the selling floor and it is ready for consumers to make their selection and take it to the check-out.  Competition from online sellers.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation
A: Wholesalers sell to retailers and retailers sell to end consumers.
Q: How do wholesalers differ from retailers?
Q: Some wholesalers ‘retail’ direct to consumers. Can you think of some examples?
A: Examples include some grocery wholesalers who sell grocery products to retailers and also to end users. Producer co-operatives such as seafood co-ops and fruit & vegetable co-ops sell direct to retailers, and they can also sell direct to consumers through their producer shops.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation
A: Pressures on wholesalers include the need for greater efficiency and for investment in Information Systems and in the automation of their warehouses. Wholesalers also need to increase their services to retailers. For example, grocery wholesalers need to provide marketing and branding for independently-owned supermarkets. Pharmaceutical wholesalers likewise to retail pharmacies.
Q: What are some other wholesaling trends and developments? Give examples.
